title: Jeremiah C. Fairfield (1817-1898): A China-Born Farmer of Troy, Maine
narrative: Jeremiah C. Fairfield was born on 20 December 1817 in the town that is today China, Kennebec County, Maine. On the day of his birth the community had not yet taken that name: the area had been settled around 1774 as Jones' Plantation, was incorporated in 1796 as Harlem, and only became the town of China on 25 June 1818 - six months after Jeremiah was born - when Harlem was joined with parts of Fairfax (now Albion) and Winslow. He thus entered the world in a young interior Maine farming town that was, almost literally, still settling on its identity, in the rolling country northeast of Augusta around the long, two-basin body of water now called China Lake.

His parents are recorded as John Sedgley Fairfield and Mary Shaw. The Fairfield surname is an old and widespread one in New England: most Maine Fairfields trace to John Fairfield, an English immigrant who settled by the late 1630s in the Salem-Wenham area of Essex County, Massachusetts, and whose descendants spread into Maine - notably the Kennebunkport (York County) line from which a great many later Fairfields, including Maine's governor and U.S. senator John Fairfield (1797-1847, son of Ichabod Fairfield and Sarah Nason), descend. It is worth being candid that this Jeremiah's specific place within that larger Fairfield tree is not established by the free sources consulted here; his connection to the governor's southern-Maine line, or to the separately named town of Fairfield in Somerset County, is not documented and should not be assumed. The names in his own household are themselves suggestive - 'John Sedgley Fairfield' and 'Mary Shaw' echo an old English Fairfield couple of the same names (recorded circa 1588-1650) - but online trees have detached that ancient link for want of a reliable source, so the recurrence of those names is offered here only as an unresolved curiosity, not as proof of descent.

Jeremiah married Sarah Chase. The Chase surname was common across central Maine and the Kennebec and Waldo county towns, but beyond her name the free documentary record assembled here is thin: a precise marriage date and place, and the names and birth years of the couple's children, are not confirmed in the sources consulted and are therefore left open rather than invented. What is clear is that Jeremiah and Sarah formed a farming household of exactly the kind that filled the interior Maine towns of their generation, raising a family on the land in a region where neighbors were often also kin and where a handful of surnames - Fairfield, Chase, and the like - recurred across the local records.

At some point in his life Jeremiah moved east from China, in Kennebec County, into Troy, in Waldo County, where he would die. Troy lies in Maine's interior a short distance to the east, and like China it had passed through several names before settling on its own: established around 1801 as Bridge's Plantation, incorporated as Kingville in 1812, then renamed Joy (1815), Montgomery (1826), and finally Troy (1826/1827), about the same time Waldo County itself was organized in 1827. The eastward and northward drift of a Kennebec-born family into the newer Waldo County farm towns fits the broad, well-documented pattern of nineteenth-century settlement in interior Maine, even though the exact year and circumstances of Jeremiah's own move are not pinned down in the free records.

By every indication Jeremiah Fairfield lived the life of a Maine farmer. Troy in the nineteenth century was a town of scattered family farms and rural neighborhoods - East, North, and West Troy, Troy Center, and a scatter of named 'corners' - with little industry beyond what soil, woodlot, and small local mills could yield. A man of his time and place would have practiced the mixed subsistence farming typical of the interior: hay, oats, potatoes, a few cattle and sheep, a kitchen garden, and home production of much that could not be bought. Troy's population tells the larger story of his working life: it peaked at 1,484 in 1850, then eroded steadily - 1,201 by 1870 and just 766 by 1900 - as the rural young left for the cities, the mills, and the open lands of the West. Jeremiah was among those who stayed and farmed through that long decline.

His adult years also spanned the Civil War, which fell heavily on rural Maine; the state sent a disproportionately large share of its men into the Union armies, and small Waldo County towns like Troy gave up soldiers, money, and grief far out of proportion to their size. Jeremiah, however, was in his mid-forties when the war broke out in 1861 - well past the usual enlistment age - and the free records consulted here do not show military service for him. The far more likely shape of his war was the home-front one shared by older farmers across Maine: keeping a farm and a community going while younger neighbors and kinsmen marched south. The war is set down here as the defining national event of his middle age, not as a documented chapter of his personal story.

Jeremiah C. Fairfield died on 7 July 1898 in Troy, Waldo County, Maine, at the age of eighty. What can be said of him with confidence is modest but solid: a man born 20 December 1817 in the freshly organized town of China (then Harlem), Kennebec County, son of John Sedgley Fairfield and Mary Shaw, husband of Sarah Chase, who settled into the farming town of Troy in Waldo County and died there in 1898 after a long life that ran nearly the full length of the nineteenth century. The richer texture of his days - the cycles of a subsistence farm, the intermarried web of central Maine's families, the shadow of the Civil War, and the slow rural depopulation around him - is drawn here from the documented history of his towns and county rather than from records naming him directly, and is offered as honest context rather than invented biography.
